reason: The theorem claims each worldline gamma_j intersects Sigma transversally at a UNIQUE point. Transversality (nonzero inner product of nabla f and gamma-dot) is correctly guaranteed by the reverse Cauchy-Schwarz inequality at each point. However, uniqueness requires that f composed with gamma is GLOBALLY monotone along the entire worldline, so that f(gamma(tau)) crosses zero exactly once. The proof sketch only establishes that d/dtau[f(gamma(tau))] = partial_gamma f . gamma-dot^gamma is nonzero at each point (local transversality). For uniqueness, one needs: (1) the sign of partial_gamma f . gamma-dot^gamma is constant along the entire worldline (not just nonzero at each point), AND (2) f(gamma(tau)) tends to +/- infinity as tau -> +/- infinity (or at least changes sign). Point (1) follows from continuity if the worldline is connected and nabla f remains timelike everywhere (both are assumed), since a continuous nonvanishing function on a connected domain has constant sign. Point (2) requires an additional assumption: that the worldline is complete (or at least that it exits every compact level set of f). Neither assumption is explicitly stated. The theorem should either assume worldlines are complete and inextendible, or explicitly state conditions ensuring f(gamma) is surjective onto R.
